English Version BelowTopic 1 – Tissue Engineering ParadigmBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)The tissue engineering paradigm integrates cells, biocompatible materials, biochemical factors like growth factors, and biophysical factors such as cyclic mechanical loading to regenerate tissues. Mind map: Paradigm → Cells (source) → Materials (scaffolds) → Biochemical (growth factors) → Biophysical (mechanical loading) → Outcomes (tissue regeneration) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Core components: cells, biocompatible materials, biochemical factors (e.g., growth factors), biophysical factors (e.g., cyclic mechanical loading). Aims to regenerate 3D tissues using a multidisciplinary approach. Cells provide the building blocks for new tissue formation. Materials act as scaffolds for cell attachment and growth. Biochemical factors stimulate cell proliferation and differentiation. Biophysical factors mimic in vivo mechanical environments to guide tissue development. Paradigm emphasizes biomimicry of natural ECM. Applications include hard tissues like bone and soft tissues like vascular. Challenges: controlling differentiation, proliferation, and immune response. Mechanical environment plays a key role in cell signaling and phenotype. Scaffolds must support nutrient diffusion and vascularization. Cyclic stretching is essential for constructs like myocardium. Integration of factors ensures synchronized tissue function. Paradigm supports both in vitro and in vivo regeneration.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Tissue Engineering Triad:Cells + Scaffolds (Materials) + Signals (Biochemical + Biophysical) → Regenerated Tissue Diagram: Paradigm Flow[Cells] --> [Seed on Scaffold] --> [Add Growth Factors] --> [Apply Mechanical Load] --> [Implant/Regenerate] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Biochemical Factors Biophysical Factors
Definition Chemical signals like growth factors Physical stimuli like mechanical loading
Role Promote proliferation/differentiation Influence shape, viability, and signaling
Examples VEGF, BMP Shear stress, cyclic stretch
Mechanism Bind receptors, activate pathways Transmit via cytoskeleton to nucleus
Exam Focus Pathway details Tensegrity integration
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Mistaking biophysical for biochemical: Examiners may ask to differentiate effects; don’t confuse mechanical loading with growth factors. Overlooking integration: Trap in questions on paradigm without mentioning all four components. Assuming all factors are equal: Biophysical often underemphasized in answers.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks CB3: Cells, Biocompatible materials, Biochemical, Biophysical – “Can’t Build Without CB3”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)What are the key components of the tissue engineering paradigm?
The paradigm includes cells for tissue building, biocompatible materials as scaffolds, biochemical factors like growth factors for signaling, and biophysical factors such as mechanical loading to mimic in vivo conditions.
Explain the role of biophysical factors in tissue engineering.
Biophysical factors like cyclic mechanical loading guide cell behavior, promote differentiation (e.g., myotube formation), and ensure functional tissue constructs, as seen in engineered heart tissue requiring stretching for synchronization.
Topic 2 – Tensegrity ModelBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)The tensegrity model describes cells as prestressed structures where tension and compression elements balance forces, enabling rapid mechanical signal transmission for mechanotransduction. Mind map: Tensegrity → Architectural (tension-compression synergy) → Cellular (actin tension, microtubules compression) → Pre-stress (contractile tension) → Force Propagation (rapid, long-distance) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Championed by Don Ingber as alternative to elastic bag model. Cells and nuclei are “hard-wired” to respond to physical stimuli via integrins. Tension elements: actin filaments; compression: microtubules. Pre-stress from actomyosin contraction balances forces. Stabilizes cell structure and morphology. Forces transmitted at ECM/cell contacts, not equally over surface. Inhibited by integrin antagonists (shear stress, strain effects). Provides basis for mechanotransduction linking physical to molecular events. Explains transmission to nucleus. Synergy between balanced tension and compression. Architectural system stabilizes by counteracting forces. Applies to natural and artificial forms (Buckminster Fuller). Prestressed structures promote long-distance force propagation. Faster than chemical signals (1000x via cytoskeleton). Contrasts with homogeneous material models where forces decay rapidly.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Tensegrity Balance:Tension (Actin) + Compression (Microtubules) + Pre-stress = Stable Network Diagram: Cellular Tensegrity[ECM] --> [Integrins] --> [Actin (Tension)] <--> [Microtubules (Compression)] --> [Nucleus]Pre-stress: Actomyosin sliding Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Tensegrity Model Elastic Bag Model
Structure Prestressed tension-compression Viscous fluid in bag
Force Transmission Rapid along cytoskeleton Slow diffusion
Response Immediate to physical stimuli Delayed chemical
Key Elements Actin, microtubules, integrins Membrane, cytosol
Applications Mechanotransduction explanation Basic cell mechanics
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Forgetting pre-stress: Trap in explaining force balance without mentioning actomyosin. Confusing elements: Mixing tension (actin) with compression (microtubules). Underestimating speed: Examiners test on why mechanical > chemical propagation.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks TAM: Tension Actin, Microtubules compression – “Tense Actors Manage”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Describe the tensegrity model in cells.
Cells maintain structure via tension (actin filaments) and compression (microtubules) elements, with pre-stress from actomyosin, allowing rapid force transmission through a hard-wired network.
How does pre-stress contribute to force propagation?
Pre-stress stiffens the cytoskeleton, enabling long-distance signal travel without rapid decay, unlike in non-prestressed models.
Topic 3 – MechanoreceptorsBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Mechanoreceptors are plasma membrane proteins that sense external mechanical signals and relay them intracellularly, including integrins, cadherins, and stretch-activated ion channels. Mind map: Mechanoreceptors → Integrins (ECM-cytoskeleton link) → Cadherins (cell-cell adhesion) → Ion Channels (ion flux on stretch) → Outcomes (signaling, gene expression) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Located in plasma membrane to sense and relay signals. Integrins: Link ECM to cytoskeleton via focal adhesions, alter cytoskeleton directly. Cadherins: Form adherens junctions for cell-cell force transmission. Stretch-activated ion channels: Selective for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-; involved in signaling and gene expression. Activated by membrane deformation; may activate or inactivate on stretch. Produce intracellular consequences like Ca2+ flux. Regulate actin polymerization and contact stability. Involved in cell signaling, transcription, translation, protein synthesis. Electrochemical gradient drives ion movement. Conformational changes with deformation. High ion selectivity. Essential for mechanotransduction. Cadherins may regulate proliferative signaling via β-catenin. Integrins transmit shear stress signals.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Ion Channel Activation:Stretch → Conformational Change → Ion Flux (e.g., Ca2+ in) → Signaling Cascade Diagram: Mechanoreceptors[External Force] --> [Integrin/Cadherin/Ion Channel] --> [Cytoskeleton/Nucleus] --> [Gene Expression] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Integrins Cadherins
Function ECM-cytoskeleton link Cell-cell adhesion
Location Focal adhesions Adherens junctions
Response Alter cytoskeleton Regulate force transmission
Signaling FAK, ERK activation β-catenin to nucleus
Examples Shear stress induction Tension stabilization
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Confusing integrins with cadherins: Trap in cell-cell vs cell-ECM questions. Ignoring ion selectivity: Examiners ask for specific ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+). Overlooking deactivation: Some channels inactivate on stretch.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks ICI: Integrins-Cell-ECM, Cadherins-Cell-Cell, Ion Channels-Ions – “I See Ions”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)What are the main types of mechanoreceptors?
Integrins for ECM linkage, cadherins for cell-cell adhesion, and stretch-activated ion channels for ion flux.
How do stretch-activated ion channels function?
They span the membrane, open on deformation allowing selective ion movement based on gradients, influencing signaling and gene expression.
Topic 4 – Integrins and Focal AdhesionsBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Integrins are transmembrane proteins mediating cell-ECM binding and force transmission, forming focal adhesions that mature under tension to activate signaling pathways. Mind map: Integrins → Ligand Binding → Focal Complexes (precursor) → Focal Adhesions (mature) → Signaling (FAK, ERK) → Cellular Responses (shape, motility) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Integrins elicit signaling similar to shear stress in endothelial cells. Transmit forces from ECM to cytoskeleton. Convert physical stimuli to bioelectrical/biochemical signals. Focal complexes: Small, punctate, at cell periphery; precursors to FAs. Move centripetally, elongate into FAs. Focal adhesions: Larger, proximal; require tension for development. Formation: Integrin occupation, clustering, tensile force exertion. Involve proteins like vinculin, talin, paxillin. Shear stress recruits FAK to leading edge. Tyrosine phosphorylation leads to ERK activation. FAK mutant blocks ERK response to stress. Integrin-ECM dynamics altered by shear. Forces travel to FAs via ECM or cytoskeleton. Essential for mechanobiology responses.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)FA Maturation:FC (Minutes) --> Tension --> FA (Larger, Proximal) Diagram: Focal Adhesion[ECM] --> [Integrin Cluster] --> [FA Proteins: FAK, Vinculin] --> [Actin] --> [Signaling: ERK] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Focal Complexes Focal Adhesions
Size/Location Small, peripheral Larger, proximal
Formation Time Minutes after contact Evolves from FCs
Role Precursor, leading edge Mature, tension-dependent
Proteins Initial integrins FAK, vinculin, talin
Movement Centripetal Stable anchor
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Mixing FC and FA: Trap in timeline or location questions. Forgetting tension role: Answers missing “tensile forces” for maturation. Ignoring signaling: Examiners test on FAK-ERK link.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks FC to FA: “Fast Cats (FCs) Become Fat Adults (FAs) Under Tension”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Differentiate focal complexes from focal adhesions.
Focal complexes are small, peripheral precursors formed minutes after ECM contact, while focal adhesions are larger, mature structures requiring tension and integrin clustering.
How do integrins transmit mechanical signals?
Through focal adhesions, where forces activate FAK and ERK pathways, converting physical stimuli to biochemical signals.
Topic 5 – Mechanosignaling PathwaysBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Mechanosignaling pathways convert mechanical stimuli at cell surfaces into intracellular responses like gene expression, involving integrins, VEGF, Wnt, and nuclear transmission. Mind map: Stimuli (Shear/Stretch) → Receptors (Integrins/Cadherins) → Pathways (FAK/ERK, VEGF, Wnt) → Nucleus (SUN proteins, chromatin) → Responses (Differentiation/Proliferation) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Mechanobiology: Responses to shear/stretch at focal adhesions. Pathways: FAK activation, ERK via tyrosine phosphorylation. Shear causes FAK recruitment and rear disappearance. VEGF: Activates Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK, phosphorylates Ets-1 for angiogenic genes. Nuclear transmission: SUN proteins link to lamina, chromatin via MARs. Nuclear actin/myosin control gene positioning and activation. Adherens junctions: Similar to FAs, dynamic link to actin. Tension at junctions stabilizes AJs, regulates β-catenin signaling. Wnt pathway: β-catenin dissociation to nucleus for gene expression. Cadherins require force for separation. Vinculin localizes to AJs, binds cytoskeletal proteins. Mechanical signals to nucleus within milliseconds. Involved in proliferation, apoptosis, differentiation. Examples: Ca2+ flux at junctions strengthens contacts.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)VEGF Pathway:VEGF --> VEGF-R2 --> Sos --> Ras-GTP --> Raf --> MEK --> ERK --> Ets-1 --> Genes (MMP-1, etc.) Diagram: Nuclear Signaling[Force] --> [Cytoskeleton] --> [SUN Proteins] --> [Lamina/Chromatin] --> [Gene Activation] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Integrin Pathways Cadherin Pathways
Activation Shear/stretch at FAs Tension at AJs
Key Proteins FAK, ERK β-catenin, vinculin
Outcome ERK activation, gene expression Proliferation via Wnt
Location Cell-ECM Cell-cell
Debate Clear force transmission Tension response under debate
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Pathway mix-up: Confusing VEGF with Wnt; examiners test sequence. Forgetting nuclear link: Trap in explaining millisecond response. Overlooking β-catenin: Answers missing nuclear translocation.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks VRRME: VEGF-R2-Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK – “Very Rapid Response Makes Everything”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Outline the VEGF kinase signaling pathway.
VEGF binds VEGF-R2, localizes Sos to activate Ras-GTP, which binds Raf to phosphorylate MEK, then ERK, leading to Ets-1 phosphorylation and angiogenic gene expression.
How do mechanical signals reach the nucleus?
Via cytoskeleton linked to SUN proteins, which connect to lamina and chromatin, enabling rapid gene activation through prestress regulation.
Topic 6 – Effects of Mechanical Forces on CellsBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Mechanical forces influence cell shape, viability, motility, differentiation, and proliferation, switching genetic programs and guiding tissue formation. Mind map: Forces → Cell Shape (spreading/deformation) → Viability (apoptosis/growth) → Motility (lamellipodia) → Differentiation (osteo/myogenic) → Proliferation (stress accumulation) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Cell shape changes from force balance; spreading if ECM stiffer than cytoskeleton. Nucleus deforms, switches apoptosis/differentiation programs. Geometric control: Micropatterns determine apoptosis vs growth via focal adhesions. Motility: Lamellipodia at stress concentrations. Differentiation: Acto-myosin contractility promotes osteogenesis. Myotube: Striations on muscle-stiffness gels. Stiffness directs lineage: Soft for neuron, medium muscle, hard bone. Cadherins: Force induces Ca2+ flux, actin polymerization. Traction microscopy: Measures force during differentiation. Proliferation: High at edges/sharp geometries; Rho inhibition abolishes. Myocardium construct: Neonatal cells + collagen, cyclic stretch for synchronization. Morphologically similar to adult tissue after 6 days. Tissue shape determines stress accumulation for growth. Mechanical precedent in differentiation.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Stiffness-Differentiation:Soft (~0.1-1 kPa) → NeuronMedium (~10 kPa) → MuscleHard (~30 kPa) → Bone Diagram: Shape-Dependent Fate[Micropattern] --> [Focal Adhesions] --> [Shape] --> [Apoptosis/Growth/Differentiation] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Shape-Dependent Viability Shape-Dependent Differentiation
Control Micropatterned ECM Geometric features/contractility
Outcome Apoptosis vs growth Osteogenesis, myogenesis
Key Factor Focal adhesions Acto-myosin tension
Examples Small patterns → apoptosis High contractility → bone
Stiffness Role N/A Matches tissue (e.g., muscle gels)
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Ignoring stiffness: Trap in differentiation without mentioning kPa values. Generalizing proliferation: High at edges, not uniform. Forgetting precedence: Mechanical changes before differentiation.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks SMDP: Shape-Motility-Differentiation-Proliferation – “Shapes Make Dolphins Play”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)How does cell shape affect viability?
On micropatterned substrates, restricted shape limits focal adhesions, promoting apoptosis, while spread shapes support growth.
Explain stiffness-directed differentiation.
Matrix stiffness mimics tissues: soft for neurons, medium for muscle, hard for bone, guiding stem cell lineage via mechanotransduction.
Topic 7 – Scaffolds Introduction and RequirementsBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Scaffolds are artificial ECMs providing 3D support for cell growth, nutrient diffusion, and tissue regeneration, with requirements for porosity, biocompatibility, and mechanical matching. Mind map: Scaffolds → Functions (support, degrade, diffuse) → Requirements (3D porous, biocompatible, surface chemistry, mechanical) → Biomimetic (mimic ECM) → Applications (bone, vascular) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Temporary/permanent artificial ECM for 3D regeneration. ECM: Macromolecules (proteins/carbs) as space fillers. 2D/3D structures; promote cell growth, degrade. Allow nutrient/waste diffusion, cell migration. Encourage blood vessel formation. Biomimetic: Apply natural methods to engineering. Examples: Trabecular for bone, triphasic for bone-ligament. Requirements: 3D porous interconnected network. Biocompatible, bioresorbable with controllable degradation. Suitable surface for attachment/proliferation/differentiation. Mechanical properties match implantation site. High porosity for cell colonization. Tunable biodegradability. Biocompatibility prevents immune reaction.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Scaffold Requirements:Porosity > 90% + Interconnectivity + Degradation Rate = Tissue Growth Match Diagram: Scaffold Functions[Cells] --> [Attach to Scaffold] --> [Proliferate/Migrate] --> [Degrade Scaffold] --> [Form Tissue] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect 2D Scaffolds 3D Scaffolds
Structure Substrate-like Volumetric
Function Basic support Full tissue regeneration
Diffusion Limited Enhanced via pores
Applications In vitro studies In vivo implants
Challenges No depth Vascularization
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Forgetting interconnectivity: Porosity alone insufficient; traps in diffusion questions. Mismatching mechanics: Answers ignoring site-specific properties. Overlooking degradation: Must parallel tissue formation.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks PBMS: Porous, Biocompatible, Mechanical match, Surface chemistry – “Please Build My Scaffold”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)What are the key requirements for scaffolds?
3D porous interconnected for growth/nutrients, biocompatible/bioresorbable matching degradation to tissue growth, suitable surface chemistry, and mechanical properties matching implantation site.
Why is biomimicry important in scaffolds?
It mimics natural ECM to tailor cell responses, effectively engineering tissues like bone (trabecular) or vascular (hydrogels).
Topic 8 – Scaffold Properties and StructuresBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Scaffold properties include composition (polymers/ceramics/metals), macrostructure, porosity/interconnectivity, and other factors like surface/volume ratio and degradation for optimal tissue integration. Mind map: Properties → Composition (natural/synthetic) → Structure (macro, pores/tubes) → Porosity (200-500μm) → Others (ratio, strength, non-toxic degradation) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Composed of polymers, ceramics, metals, composites. Match tissue: Polymers for soft, metals/ceramics for hard. Porous for cell entry, large surface area, interconnected. Composition: Pure non-organic, composites, organic; solid/gel. Macrostructure: External geometry congruent to tissue. Porosity: Isolated/interconnected; improves nutrition. Pore sizes 200-500μm for vascular ingrowth. Tubular structures beneficial for bone. High surface/volume ratio for cell attachment. Mechanical strength for load-bearing (cartilage/bone). Degradation parallels tissue formation, retains integrity. No toxic products released. Challenges: Control differentiation, porosity vs strength. Processing: Consistent technologies.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Porosity Calculation:Porosity (%) = (Void Volume / Total Volume) x 100; Ideal >80% Diagram: Porosity[Bulk Material] --> [Introduce Pores/Tubes] --> [Interconnect] --> [Nutrient Flow + Cell Migration] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Isolated Pores Interconnected Pores
Nutrition Limited diffusion Enhanced flow
Cell Survival Poor in depths Better in regions
Applications Basic scaffolds Advanced regeneration
Vascularization Low High (200-500μm)
Strength Higher May compromise
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Pore size error: Confusing vascular (200-500μm) with general. Ignoring ratio: Trap in attachment without surface/volume. Degradation toxicity: Forgetting non-toxic requirement.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks PMPO: Porosity, Macrostructure, Properties, Other – “Please Make Pores Open”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Describe scaffold porosity and interconnectivity.
Porosity allows cell growth and nutrient transport; interconnectivity ensures diffusion to deeper areas, with 200-500μm pores ideal for vascular ingrowth.
What other properties are crucial for scaffolds?
High surface/volume ratio for attachment, mechanical strength for load-bearing, tunable degradation without toxicity, matching tissue formation rate.
Topic 9 – Types of ScaffoldsBig Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Scaffolds are classified by biocompatibility as bioinert (minimal interaction), bioactive (interacts with tissue), or bioresorbable (dissolves and replaced by tissue), and by material as natural or synthetic. Mind map: Types → Biocompatibility (Inert/Active/Resorbable) → Material (Natural/Synthetic) → Examples (Ti inert, HA active, TCP resorbable) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Bioinert: Minimal tissue interaction (e.g., Titanium). Bioactive: Bonds to bone/soft tissue (e.g., HA). Bioresorbable: Dissolves, replaced by tissue (e.g., Tricalcium phosphate). Classification of bioceramics: Inert (dental implants), bioactive (HA coating), surface active (bioglass), resorbable (TCP). Natural: Biologically active, complex structure, high cost, pathogenic risks. Synthetic: Modifiable, cheap, reproducible, but degradation/biocompatibility concerns. Natural examples: Collagen, agarose, heparin. Synthetic: Metals, ceramics, polymers. Natural suffice most criteria but limited modification. Synthetic enable specific properties and shapes. Bioinert: Fibrous capsule formation. Bioactive: Bonding within 30 days. Resorbable: Disappears 10-30 days. Composites combine fillers in matrix.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)Biocompatibility Classes:Bioinert → No BondBioactive → Bond (30 days)Bioresorbable → Resorb (10-30 days) Diagram: Bioceramics[A: Inert Implant] [B: HA Coating] [C: Bioglass] [D: TCP Resorbable] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Bioinert Bioactive Bioresorbable
Interaction Minimal Bonds to tissue Dissolves/replaced
Examples Titanium Hydroxyapatite Tricalcium phosphate
Tissue Response Fibrous capsule Bone ingrowth Tissue advancement
Duration Permanent Stable bond Temporary
Applications Stents, instruments Coatings, restorations Bone grafts
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Misclassifying: Confusing bioactive with bioresorbable; examiners use examples. Ignoring risks: Natural materials’ pathogenic issues. Overstating synthetic: Forgetting toxicity concerns.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks IAR: Inert-Active-Resorbable – “I Am Resorbable”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Compare bioinert and bioresorbable scaffolds.
Bioinert have minimal interaction and remain permanent (e.g., Ti), while bioresorbable dissolve and are replaced by tissue (e.g., TCP).
Why choose natural over synthetic materials?
Natural offer biological activity and complex structures but at higher cost and risk; synthetic are modifiable and reproducible but may have degradation issues.
Topic 10 – Scaffold Materials (Metals and Ceramics)Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)Scaffold materials include metals (e.g., Ti, Mg) for strength and ceramics (e.g., HA, bioglass) for bioactivity, each with advantages in biocompatibility but limitations in toxicity or mechanics. Mind map: Materials → Metals (Ti scaffolds, Mg alloys) → Ceramics (CaP, HA, bioglass) → Properties (corrosion resistance, resorbable) → Applications (orthopedic, dental) Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points) Metals: High strength, but lack biological recognition; surface modification needed. Possible toxic ion release from corrosion/wear. Examples: 316L SS for stents, Ti alloys for bone replacement. Ti: High strength, light, corrosion resistant, biocompatible; porous for fixation. Bioinert, slow bone bonding; BMP-loading enhances. Mg alloys: Bioresorbable, osteoconductive, but rapid dissolution risk (hypermagnesia). Ceramics: Refractory, inert, biocompatible; hard, brittle. CaP: Bioactive, resorbable; poor mechanics, used as coatings. HA: Similar to bone mineral, supports ingrowth; for augmentation. Bioglass: Bonds to tissues, composition SiO2-CaO-Na2O; bone cement. Bioactivity regions: A (bonding), B (inert), C (resorbable), D (non-glass). Resorbable: Replaced by bone without mechanical loss. Metals for hard tissues, polymers for soft. Challenges: Toxicity, corrosion for metals; brittleness for ceramics.
Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)HA Formula: Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2TCP: Ca3(PO4)2 Diagram: Metal Scaffold[Ti Porous] --> [Cell Ingrowth] --> [Bone Fixation] + [BMP] --> [Enhanced Regeneration] Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)
Aspect Metals Ceramics
Properties Strong, deformable Hard, brittle
Biocompatibility Bioinert, modifiable Bioactive/resorbable
Examples Ti, Mg HA, bioglass
Limitations Toxic ions, corrosion Poor load-bearing
Applications Bone plates, stents Coatings, bone cement
Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps Forgetting modifications: Metals need coatings for bioactivity. Mg toxicity: Trap in resorption without mentioning excretion/hyper-magnesia. HA vs TCP: Confusing non-resorbable HA with resorbable TCP.
Mnemonics / Memory Hooks TMC: Ti-Mg-Ceramics – “Tough Metals Ceramics”.
Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)Discuss titanium as a scaffold material.
Ti offers strength, lightness, and biocompatibility; porous structures allow ingrowth, but bioinert nature requires BMP or coatings for better bonding.
Compare metals and ceramics for scaffolds.
Metals provide strength but risk toxicity; ceramics are bioactive and resorbable but brittle, suited for coatings rather than load-bearing.
